name: Ecosystem replacement surveillance pass for June 3, 2026

scope.question: What changed for Ecosystem Replacement since the June 3, 2026 bootstrap publication, and does any change justify a public update?

summary: No material post-publication delta was found. NCT05598112 remains recruiting with no results section, NCT06649981 remains active-not-recruiting with no results section, and the ARMOR protocol remains the latest indexed trial publication tied to those direct older-adult FMT aging programs. PubMed searches for same-day post-baseline FMT aging, frailty, older-adult, or sarcopenia records returned no new records. A DDW 2026/UTMB report on autologous preserved-youth microbiome transfer in aging mice was reviewed as context, but it predates the June 3 publication window, is conference/news coverage rather than a peer-reviewed full source, and does not alter the current public stage, confidence, momentum, or support map.

materiality_decision.rationale: The only direct older-adult aging trials checked still have no posted efficacy or safety results, and no new PubMed-indexed record entered the delta window. The pre-window DDW animal report may be worth watching for a peer-reviewed paper, but preliminary animal conference coverage does not change an already animal-led, low-confidence human_biomarker_signal outlook.

search_log.summary: Record remains RECRUITING, last posted update December 31, 2025, with estimated primary completion December 31, 2026 and no results section.
Record remains ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING, last posted update April 20, 2026, with estimated primary completion September 30, 2026 and no results section.

search_log.query: NCT05598112 OR NCT06649981 OR ARMOR fecal microbiota aging
search_log.summary: The only indexed match was PMID 41629813, the already published ARMOR trial protocol included in the baseline.

search_log.query: (fecal microbiota transplantation OR fecal microbiota transplant OR FMT) AND (aging OR ageing OR frailty OR older adults OR sarcopenia), same-day delta
search_log.summary: Date-filtered checks for June 3, 2026 publication or Entrez-entry dates returned no records in the delta window.

search_log.query: preserved youthful microbiome aging mice liver cancer FMT DDW 2026
search_log.summary: Reviewed pre-window DDW 2026 coverage of autologous preserved-youth microbiome transfer in aged mice; treated as watchlist context rather than a material post-publication delta.

excluded_sources.title: Restoration of a youthful gut microbiome reduced liver aging and suppressed tumorigenesis in older mice
excluded_sources.reason: Conference/news coverage predates the June 3, 2026 publication window and is not a peer-reviewed full source. The reported aged-mouse liver signals do not change the current animal-led public interpretation or justify adding support-map evidence without the full study.

excluded_sources.title: Turning back the clock on the microbiome is key to preventing liver cancer and aging, study finds
excluded_sources.reason: Institutional news coverage describes the same DDW presentation and does not add independently published outcomes.

excluded_sources.title: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of fecal microbiota transplantation from young physically active donors to promote resilient aging: clinical trial protocol (ARMOR study)
excluded_sources.reason: Already included in the published ecosystem-replacement baseline as protocol-level, no-results trial context.

notes: Triage selected this track as the next distinct surveillance target after microbiome-composition-modulation.
The latest coverage assessment recommends ordinary surveillance, with special attention to NCT05598112, NCT06649981, and related publications.
The direct older-adult FMT aging evidence remains trial-watch activity without posted outcome results.
The DDW 2026 autologous microbiome mouse-liver report should be revisited if a peer-reviewed paper, abstract record, or clinical trial registration appears.
next_actions: Continue ordinary surveillance for ecosystem replacement on the next rotation unless trial results, a peer-reviewed DDW follow-up, or a safety signal appears.
Keep coverage repair as a separate future task for recent-review and seminal-anchor triangulation if the track is revisited before older-adult trial results post.
